title: Chevron pattern marbling
subtitle: The herringbone variant of the combed marbling pattern
excerpt: The chevron is made by combing a nonpareil or straight-raked pattern in alternate directions across the tray. Each alternating comb pass creates a V-shape that, repeated across the sheet, produces a herringbone. The pattern was popular in European bookbinding from the late seventeenth century onward.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation 1894), public domain. Halfer documents the chevron as a variant of the combed patterns. C.W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853/1881), public domain: Woolnough's earlier text confirms the pattern's European bookbinding use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A closely and evenly raked pattern produced by combing the size in one direction with narrow spacing, then re-combing at 90° perpendicular. The nonpareil is the starting point for several more complex combed patterns.

content.text: The chevron is built on a

: a straight nonpareil combed pattern: and then worked further with a fine-tined comb moving in alternating directions. Each alternate-direction pass creates a set of V-shapes; repeated across the tray width, they lock together into the herringbone. The chevron is the nonpareil's most direct descendant in the combed-pattern family.

attrs.heading: Safety: marbling chemistry
attrs.body: Carrageenan size and acrylic pigments are non-toxic at marbling concentrations. Dispose of used size by pouring it outside or diluting and draining into a household drain in small quantities. Do not pour large quantities of alum-mordant rinse water into a septic system: dilute heavily first. Wash all tools and hands thoroughly after the session.

items.name: Carrageenan size (slug: carrageenan)
items.description: Prepared the day before: 1 teaspoon per litre of water, blended, rested 24 hours. Surface must be clear and smooth before use.

items.name: Marbling acrylic, liquid (slug: marbling-acrylic-liquid)
items.description: Four colours. Prepared with 1-2 drops of ox-gall per 30 ml of colour to control spread.

items.name: Marbling comb (slug: marbling-comb)
items.description: A fine comb: tines approximately 10-12 mm apart: for the second combing pass.

items.name: Alum-mordanted paper (slug: marbling-paper-alum)
items.description: Prepared by brushing both sides of cartridge paper with a 15% alum solution and drying. Must be used within 24 hours of mordanting.

content.text: Drop four colours in alternating rows across the length of the tray: one stripe of colour A, one of colour B, one of colour C, one of colour D, then repeat. The stripes should be roughly equal width: 20-30 mm each for a standard tray. Use the eyedropper for controlled placement.
First comb pass: the nonpareil base

Draw the wide comb (25-30 mm tine spacing) across the tray from left to right in one smooth pass, pulling the colour stripes into fine parallel lines. This is the first stage of the nonpareil: the "width" comb. Then draw the fine comb (10-12 mm tine spacing) from top to bottom of the tray in one pass, perpendicular to the first. The result should be a compact, regularly spaced crosshatched pattern: the standard nonpareil.

Now draw the fine comb from top to bottom again, but this time alternate the direction: first tine-pass goes top to bottom; second tine-pass goes bottom to top; third goes top to bottom again. Space the passes so they occur at 15-20 mm intervals across the tray width. Each alternating pass creates a V-shape in the existing nonpareil lines. The result, when the pattern is viewed as a whole, is the characteristic chevron herringbone.

Lower the mordanted paper face-down onto the size in one smooth movement, holding it at a bow and releasing from the centre outward. Leave for 30-40 seconds. Lift by one corner, rinse under running water, and hang to dry. The chevron pattern should be clean and regular. Irregular V-shapes indicate that the comb passes were not evenly spaced; work with a ruler guide on the tray edge for the next batch.
